results.title: I’m HOOKED on Claude Code Hooks: Advanced Agentic Coding
results.content:
. 🎥 VIDEO REFERENCES: Principled AI Coding:  Claude Code Hooks Quickstart:  Claude Code Hooks Anthropic Docs:  UV Single File Scripts Breakdown: Image 20•Single File Agents.Python Scripts with As... WHAT YOU’LL SEE INSIDE: 
   How these 5 key Hooks give you surgical control over Claude Code, giving you next level observability and control. 
   Real text to speech notifications powered by elevenlabs (or openai or your local device), so your agents literally tell you when a top level task or an agent sub-task is done. 
   A full observability stack for ai coding: pre-tool, post-tool, stop, notification, and sub-agent hooks—all logged and organized for rapid debugging. [...] KEY TAKEAWAYS: 
   Agentic coding ≠ “just chat”; with claude code and its hooks you control context, model, prompt like never before. 
   Parallel ai coding means spinning up multiple Claude 4 sub-agents that cooperate (or compete) while you grab coffee. 
   Block, log, notify, speak: plug into any point in the lifecycle of your agentic coding workflow. 
   Text to speech feedback isn’t fluff—it’s the missing UX layer for unattended long-running jobs. 
   Principles beat tools, but right now Claude Code is the fastest path to production-grade Agentic Coding. [...] Why indydevdan calls Claude Code the new engineering primitive and how cursor is scrambling to keep up so much so that they've hired the creators of Claude Code. 
   Step-by-step breakdown of UV single-file scripts that glue anthropic, openai, and custom hooks together for unstoppable agentic coding.

results.content:
### ​ Track subagent activity

Use `SubagentStop` hooks to monitor when subagents finish their work. See the full input type in the TypeScript and Python SDK references. This example logs a summary each time a subagent completes: [...] = ClaudeAgentOptions( hooks={"SubagentStop": [HookMatcher(hooks=[subagent_tracker])]}  hooks ={"SubagentStop": [HookMatcher(hooks =[subagent_tracker])]})) [...] How hooks work

# Intercept and control agent behavior with hooks

results.title: Claude Code Hooks: The Deterministic Control Layer for AI Agents - Dotzlaw Team
results.content:
Figure 10 - Observability pipeline architecture where all hook events feed into an event forwarding layer and real-time dashboard for monitoring agent activity and decisions.

Figure 10 - The Observability Pipeline: Every hook event feeds a monitoring system. Agent intentions (PreToolUse), results (PostToolUse), context loss (PreCompact), session lifecycle (SessionStart/Stop), and team composition (SubagentStart/Stop) all flow into a real-time dashboard. When an agent swarm misbehaves, this event log is your primary debugging tool.

`1

{

2

"hooks": {

3

"PreToolUse": [{

4

"matcher": "",

5

"hooks": [{

6

"type": "command",

7

"command": "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/hooks/observability/send_event.py --event pre_tool_use"

8

}]

9

}],

10

"PostToolUse": [{

11

"matcher": "", [...] ### Pattern 3: Observability Pipeline#

The observability pattern uses hooks across all event types to feed a monitoring system. Every `PreToolUse` forwards the agent’s intention. Every `PostToolUse` forwards the result. `PreCompact` captures what context is being lost. `SubagentStart` and `SubagentStop` track team composition.

`PreToolUse`
`PostToolUse`
`PreCompact`
`SubagentStart`
`SubagentStop`

This pattern provides full lifecycle coverage across all 13 hook events, feeding a real-time dashboard that visualizes agent behavior patterns.

Figure 10 - Observability pipeline architecture where all hook events feed into an event forwarding layer and real-time dashboard for monitoring agent activity and decisions. [...] Claude Code Hooks are user-defined shell commands that execute at specific points in Claude Code’s lifecycle . They are not prompts. They are not suggestions. They are system-level interceptors that guarantee certain actions always happen (safety checks, quality validation, observability logging, and workflow enforcement) regardless of what the agent’s reasoning chain looks like.

This article covers how hooks work, the complete event system, the three hook types, and the architectural patterns that make agent teams safe and reliable in production.

## How Hooks Work#

results.content:
### ​ Hooks in skills and agents

`Stop`
`SubagentStop`
`PreToolUse`
`Bash`
`---
name: secure-operations
description: Perform operations with security checks
hooks:
 PreToolUse:
 - matcher: "Bash"
 hooks:
 - type: command
 command: "./scripts/security-check.sh"
---`

### ​ The `/hooks` menu

`/hooks`
`/hooks`
`command`
`prompt`
`agent`
`http`
`mcp_tool`
`[type]`
`User`
`~/.claude/settings.json`
`Project`
`.claude/settings.json`
`Local`
`.claude/settings.local.json`
`Plugin`
`hooks/hooks.json`
`Session`
`Built-in`

### ​ Disable or remove hooks

`"disableAllHooks": true`
`disableAllHooks`
`disableAllHooks`
`disableAllHooks`

## ​ Hook input and output

`/dev/tty`
`/dev/tty`
`systemMessage`
`terminalSequence`

### ​ Common input fields [...] | Field | Description |
 --- |
| `agent_id` | Unique identifier for the subagent. Present only when the hook fires inside a subagent call. Use this to distinguish subagent hook calls from main-thread calls. |
| `agent_type` | Agent name (for example, `"Explore"` or `"security-reviewer"`). Present when the session uses `--agent` or the hook fires inside a subagent. For subagents, the subagent’s type takes precedence over the session’s `--agent` value. For custom subagents, this is the `name` field from the agent’s frontmatter, not the filename. | [...] #### ​ Notification input

`message`
`title`
`notification_type`
`{
 "session_id": "abc123",
 "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
 "cwd": "/Users/...",
 "hook_event_name": "Notification",
 "message": "Claude needs your permission",
 "title": "Permission needed",
 "notification_type": "permission_prompt"
}`
`systemMessage`
